questions.question: When using Supabase Realtime in React Native, why must you call supabase.removeChannel(channel) in the useEffect cleanup, and what happens without it?
options: removeChannel is needed only for premium Supabase plans — free plans auto-cleanup channels.

Without removeChannel cleanup: (1) The Supabase channel (WebSocket subscription) continues running after the component unmounts, consuming connection resources and delivering events to a function with a stale closure over old state setters. (2) If the component remounts (e.g., navigating back to the chat screen), a NEW channel is created WITHOUT removing the old one — two subscriptions now deliver duplicate messages. Over time: memory leak, duplicate messages, and hitting Supabase's concurrent channel limits.
removeChannel cancels any in-flight database queries from the channel.
removeChannel is only needed in React Native — React web apps auto-cleanup Supabase channels.
questions.answer: Without removeChannel cleanup: (1) The Supabase channel (WebSocket subscription) continues running after the component unmounts, consuming connection resources and delivering events to a function with a stale closure over old state setters. (2) If the component remounts (e.g., navigating back to the chat screen), a NEW channel is created WITHOUT removing the old one — two subscriptions now deliver duplicate messages. Over time: memory leak, duplicate messages, and hitting Supabase's concurrent channel limits.
questions.explanation: Supabase free tier allows 200 concurrent realtime connections per project. An app that doesn't removeChannel() on unmount creates a new channel on each remount — 10 navigation cycles = 10 channels. At 200 users doing this: 2000 channels, hitting the limit and blocking new real-time subscriptions for other users.

questions.question: Why is Supabase Edge Functions the right place to send push notifications (instead of the React Native app directly), even though the Expo Push API is callable from anywhere?
options: Expo Push API only accepts requests from server-side environments, not mobile clients.

Calling Expo Push API from the mobile app means: the push sending logic is part of the app bundle (exposable, modifiable); the app must know all participants' push tokens (requires fetching them → privacy leak where user A can see user B's token); no reliable way to send notifications when the triggering user is offline. Server-side (Edge Functions): tokens are never exposed to clients, business logic runs securely, notifications fire reliably as a DB trigger regardless of who is online, and backend can add rate limiting and delivery tracking.
Edge Functions are faster than mobile apps at sending HTTP requests to external APIs.
The Expo Push API blocks requests from React Native apps due to CORS restrictions.
questions.answer: Calling Expo Push API from the mobile app means: the push sending logic is part of the app bundle (exposable, modifiable); the app must know all participants' push tokens (requires fetching them → privacy leak where user A can see user B's token); no reliable way to send notifications when the triggering user is offline. Server-side (Edge Functions): tokens are never exposed to clients, business logic runs securely, notifications fire reliably as a DB trigger regardless of who is online, and backend can add rate limiting and delivery tracking.
questions.explanation: Security principle: push tokens are like phone numbers — only your backend should know them. Client-side sending would require your app to fetch all recipient tokens from the DB, which violates privacy (why would user A's app need user B's push token?). Server-side: user A's app sends 'new message' event; the server looks up B's token and sends the notification.

questions.question: What is Supabase Row Level Security (RLS), and why is it essential before calling supabase.storage.from().upload() from a React Native app?
options: RLS limits the maximum file size that can be uploaded via the Supabase Storage SDK.

Without RLS, any authenticated user can read, modify, or delete any other user's files and database rows using your public Supabase credentials (Supabase URL + anon key are in your app bundle and visible to anyone who decompiles it). RLS policies are evaluated server-side and restrict what each authenticated user can access. For storage: RLS policy 'users can only upload to their own folder (userId/*)' prevents user A from overwriting user B's avatar. For chat messages: RLS ensures users can only read messages from chats they're members of.
RLS is only needed for multi-tenant enterprise apps — single-user apps don't require it.
RLS is configured client-side in the Supabase JS SDK via filter conditions.
questions.answer: Without RLS, any authenticated user can read, modify, or delete any other user's files and database rows using your public Supabase credentials (Supabase URL + anon key are in your app bundle and visible to anyone who decompiles it). RLS policies are evaluated server-side and restrict what each authenticated user can access. For storage: RLS policy 'users can only upload to their own folder (userId/*)' prevents user A from overwriting user B's avatar. For chat messages: RLS ensures users can only read messages from chats they're members of.
questions.explanation: Critical: Supabase's anon key is designed to be public — it has no permissions by default (or limited permissions you define with RLS). The service_role key is secret and bypasses RLS — NEVER include the service_role key in your React Native app. service_role belongs only in your backend (Edge Functions, Node.js server).
